elements.sent-2411-001: We study a system of drift-diffusion PDEs for a potentially infinite number of incompressible phases, subject to a joint pointwise volume constraint.
elements.sent-2411-002: Our analysis is based on the interpretation as a collection of coupled Wasserstein gradient flows or, equivalently, as a gradient flow in the space of couplings under a `fibered' Wasserstein distance.
elements.sent-2411-003: We prove existence of weak solutions, long-time asymptotics, and stability with respect to the mass distribution of the phases, including the discrete to continuous limit.
elements.sent-2411-004: A key step is to establish convergence of the product of pressure gradient and density, jointly over the infinite number of phases.
elements.sent-2411-005: The underlying energy functional is the objective of entropy regularized optimal transport, which allows us to interpret the model as the relaxation of the classical Angenent--Haker--Tannenbaum (AHT) scheme to the entropic setting.
elements.sent-2411-006: However, in contrast to the AHT scheme's lack of convergence guarantees, the relaxed scheme is unconditionally convergent.
elements.sent-2411-007: We conclude with numerical illustrations of the main results.

elements.sent-2411-094: For $Z_1 \subset \R^d, Z_2\subset \R^{d'}$ compact sets and $\rho_1\in \prob(Z_1),\rho_2\in \prob(Z_2)$ two probability measures, $\Gamma(\rho_1, \rho_2)$ denotes the set of couplings or transport plans between $\rho_1$ and $\rho_2$: \begin{equation}\label{eq:coupling} \Gamma(\rho_1, \rho_2) \assign \{ \gamma\in\prob(Z_1\times Z_2) \ \mid \ \proj_1 \gamma = \rho_1, \quad \proj_2 \gamma = \rho_2 \}\end{equation} where the maps $\proj_1$ and $\proj_2$ denote the projections of measures on $Z_1\times Z_2$ to its marginals, i.e. \begin{equation}(\proj_1 \gamma)(S_1) \assign \gamma(S_1 \times Z_2) \qquad \tn{and} \qquad (\proj_2 \gamma)(S_2) \assign \gamma(Z_1 \times S_2)\end{equation} for $\gamma \in \prob(Z_1\times Z_2)$, $S_1 \subset Z_1$, $S_2 \subset Z_2$ measurable.

elements.sent-2411-362: In this Section we showcase with numerical simulations the main theoretical results discussed above.
elements.sent-2411-363: Our numerical experiments are based on solving the minimizing movement scheme \eqref{eq:JKO-scheme , or more precisely its formulation as a convex optimization problem \eqref{eq:primal , using the Chambolle-Pock primal-dual method \cite{Chambolle2011 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-364: Our approach differs from traditional numerical approaches to simulate multiphase porous media flows (see for instance~\cite{CE97, AS79, EHM03 ) as it builds on the variational interpretation of the problem rather than on a direct discretization of the partial differential equations~\eqref{eq:PDE1 --\eqref{eq:PDE2 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-365: Let us mention the contribution~\cite{CGLM19 where an augmented Lagrangian approach extending the ideas of~\cite{BCL16 to the context of multiphase porous media flows was compared to the more classical (and non-variational) phase-by-phase upstream mobility finite volume scheme~\cite{EHM03 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-366: To keep the figures intuitive and simple we fix $X = Y = [0,1]$, $V(x,y) = |x-y|^2/2$, and $\mu = \dd x$ the Lebesgue measure on $X$.
elements.sent-2411-368: Unless otherwise stated we discretize $\mu$ to a set of $M = 256$ equispaced points with equal weights in $[0,1]$.
elements.sent-2411-369: We set the second marginal $\nu$ likewise to a sum of $N\in \{4, 16, 64, 256\}$ equispaced dirac deltas with equal weights.
elements.sent-2411-370: For the initialization $\rho^0$ we choose either the product initialization $\rho^0 = \mu \otimes \nu$ or the flipped initialization $\rho^0 = (\id, 1 - \id)_\sharp \mu$, each discretized appropriately.
elements.sent-2411-372: For smaller values of $\tau$ we encounter discretization artefacts that hinder the convergence of the JKO step: When $X$ is discretized, the minimal cost for moving a particle horizontally becomes quantized, since it must move by at least one pixel, and it may therefore be more expensive than the potential gain by following a small $X$-gradient of $V$, especially when $\tau$ is very small.
elements.sent-2411-373: To alleviate this issue one could use entropic regularization in the transport term $\wassF$ (see e.g.~\cite{entropic-schemes ); however in this work we prefer to illustrate faithfully the behavior of the minimizing movement without introducing additional terms.}
